CSCE 111 Syllabus

Section 501 (10917)

Introduction to Computer Science Concepts and Programming

Fall 2025 - College Station

Course Information

End Time: 09:15AM

Credit Hours:  4

Instructor Details

Muhammad Mahmood

Email:  suleman.mahmood@tamu.edu

Office:  PETR 109

Phone:  9798452741

Office Hours

See course calendar on canvas

Course Description

Computation to enhance problem solving abilities; understanding how people

communicate with computers, and how computing affects society; computational

thinking; software design principles, including algorithm design, data

representation, abstraction, modularity, structured and object-oriented

programming, documentation, testing, portability, and maintenance;

understanding programs’ abilities and limitations; development and execution

programs.

Special Course Designation

None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Use a text editor to program Java code.

Use a Java compiler to produce executable software.

Identify the basic syntax and semantics of the Java programming language.

Use appropriate commenting, layout, and naming to communicate code intent

Understand the primitive data types built into the Java language.

Understand the concepts of Java classes and objects.

Understand the concepts of lifetime, scope, and the initialization mechanism of

variables.

Implement Java code branching using if or switch statements.

Program loops with while, for, and do statements.

Make use of arrays to store and process lists of data.

Assemble data and methods into classes following the software engineering

principles of encapsulation and data hiding.

Organize Java code following the software engineering principles of modularity

and abstraction.

Create Java I/O interfaces

Read, interpret, analyze, and explain introductory Java programs.

Test and evaluate introductory Java programs

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

Assemble data and methods into classes following the software engineering

principles of encapsulation and data hiding.

Organize Java code following the software engineering principles of modularity

and abstraction.

Component Specific Policies

Class Activities

Class activities will be graded based on participation rather than correctness.

They will only be available for completion during class time.

In case of excused absences, the activity will be marked excused. Canvas

processes excused absences by increasing the weight of the unexcused activities.

Three unexcused absences are allowed during the semester. This will be

processed at the end of the semester.

Lab Assignments

Labs are going to be group activities where attendance is required to earn full

credit.

Groups will be assigned in Canvas and will be changed every 4 weeks.

If you are absent from lab, you can complete the assignment individually with a

20% penalty.

In case of excused absences, lab assignment can be completed individually

without penalty.

Exam 1

At the end of the semester, Exam 1 score will be replaced by average of all three

exams if the average is higher.

This policy allows you to partially offset a low score.

Exam 2

At the end of the semester, Exam 2 score will be replaced by average of all three

exams if the average is higher.

20% penalty.

In case of excused absences, lab assignment can be completed individually

without penalty.

Exam 1

At the end of the semester, Exam 1 score will be replaced by average of all three

exams if the average is higher.

This policy allows you to partially offset a low score.

Exam 2

At the end of the semester, Exam 2 score will be replaced by average of all three

exams if the average is higher.

This policy allows you to partially offset a low score.

Regrade Requests

If you want to challenge any grading, you must do so within one week of grade

release. Regrade period for assignments published after week 13 will be shorter

and will be announced at the time of grade release. There will be no regrading

period for the final exam.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late submissions are only allowed for Homeworks.

There will be 10% per day penalty for late submissions.

There will be no late submission for the final homework of the semester.

Course Schedule

Weekly Topics

Weekly Topics

Week

Topic

1

Course Introduction, Variables and Printing

2

Numeric data types, Math library, Characters, Defining simple methods

3

Strings, Formatted Output, Boolean Type and Basic Conditionals

4

Multiple conditions, condition order, nested conditions, loop basics

5

For Loops, Exam 1.

6

Arrays and for loops. Loops patterns with arrays.

7

Multi-dimensional arrays and Debugging.

8

Introduction to Objects.

9

Objects with methods, Inheritance.

10

Exam 2. Dynamic dispatch and access control in object-oriented

programming.

11

Exceptions. Exceptions control flow and defining exceptions.

12

HTML, JavaScript and CSS

13

Graphical User Interfaces (GUI)

14

Graphical User Interfaces (GUI) continued

15

Files and introduction to object-oriented design.

16

Final Exam

Homework Deadlines

Homeworks will be released on Monday. Typical deadline for the Homeworks will be

Sunday night. Any change will be announced through canvas.

Lab Assignment Deadlines

Lab assignments will be released at the start of your weekly lab on Fridays. The deadline

for the lab assignment will be the following Monday night. The assignments are designed

to be completed within the lab session, but the deadline allows you to seek help if you

run into any problems. Any changes will be announced through canvas.

Exam Dates

Exam 1: Thursday, Sep 25, 2025. In class.

Exam 2: Friday, Oct 31, 2025, In lab.

Final Exam: Dec 12, 2025. 1:00 p.m. - 3:00 p.m.

Final exam times are based on the  TAMU official final exam schedule . If there is an

run into any problems. Any changes will be announced through canvas.

Exam 2: Friday, Oct 31, 2025, In lab.

Final Exam: Dec 12, 2025. 1:00 p.m. - 3:00 p.m.

error here, we will adhere to the TAMU official schedule.

Canvas

Dashboard for the entire course. This will link out to appropriate resources.

Campuswire

Campuswire will be the main forum to get help with homework and lab

activities.

You will be added to the discussion forum at the start of the semester.

Whenever possible, post your questions publicly so that all students can

benefit from it. Please do not post your code and solutions in public posts.

Public posts can also be answered by other students so you should have a

shorter response time for them.

We will endorse good student responses.

You have the option to post anonymously. Your post will only be anonymous

to students. Instructors will be able to see your name and id on the posts.

Please be respectful.

Private messages can be posed to all instructors or an individual instructor.

Private messages to individual instructors are more likely to be overlooked

and should not be used. Send an email directly to the instructor.

Posts directed to instructors that would benefit the entire class and should

be visible to all students will be updated so that the post is visible by the

entire class.

Google Drive

Google folder will be used to share lecture notes and in-class examples. You must be

logged into your TAMU Google account to access these materials or submit Google

Forms. Your TAMU Google account will have to be the default account in your browser,

or you can open an incognito window and log in from there. It might take up to 24 hours

after registering for the class to be able to access materials in the Google shared folder.

Zoom

Zoom will be used for online office hours, and meetings with instructors and teaching

assistants. Links will be provided as needed.

AI Statement

This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students

themselves. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any substantive

portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write

assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT.
